perturbation.original_text: Client will comply in all material respects with applicable state and federal consumer credit reporting, privacy and similar laws in connection with its use of the Interface.
perturbation.changed_text: Client will comply with all consumer credit reporting, privacy, and similar laws as TrueLink interprets them in connection with its use of the Interface.
perturbation.explanation: This change gives TrueLink the power to define what laws Client must follow, conflicting with the established legal definitions and potentially leading to Client unknowingly violating regulations based on TrueLink's interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FCRA clearly outlines consumer rights and credit reporting agency responsibilities. Allowing TrueLink to dictate legal compliance contradicts the FCRA's specific provisions and standardized interpretations of these laws, making the contract unenforceable in this aspect.

perturbation.justification: The changed text allows TrueLink to interpret consumer credit reporting laws, which directly contradicts the intent of laws like the FCRA. By allowing TrueLink's interpretation to dictate compliance, the client may unknowingly violate regulations. The original text mandates compliance with applicable state and federal laws in all material respects. The modification introduces a standard of compliance based on TrueLink's interpretation which introduces a substantial conflict with the law.

perturbation.original_text: TrueLink agrees that the costs for any Hosting Services will not increase for a period of one year from the date of this Agreement and that rates charged for Hosting Services will not exceed that charged by TrueLink to any other party.
perturbation.changed_text: TrueLink reserves the right to alter costs for Hosting Services with 30 days' notice and may offer different rates to other parties at its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This change contradicts the original agreement's fixed pricing and equal treatment clauses, introducing financial uncertainty and potential discrimination.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1655 states that 'Commonly accepted meaning of words governs interpretation.' Allowing arbitrary price changes and discriminatory pricing undermines the principle of reasonable expectation and fair dealing implied in contracts, which is generally upheld under California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1655. Stipulations which are necessary to make a contract reasonable, or conformable to usage, are implied, in respect to matters concerning which the contract manifests no contrary intention. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows TrueLink to alter costs and offer different rates, directly contradicting the original agreement's fixed pricing and equal treatment clauses. The Law Explanation indicates this undermines the principle of reasonable expectation and fair dealing. Snippet 1 (CIV 1655) states that stipulations necessary to make a contract reasonable are implied, in respect to matters concerning which the contract manifests no contrary intention. The changed text introduces terms that deviate from the original contract's intentions, creating a clear contradiction by undermining reasonable expectations of fixed pricing.

perturbation.original_text: In any action brought under this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover its actual costs and attorney fees pursuant to California Civil Code section 1717 and all other litigation costs, including expert witness fees, and all actual attorney fees and litigation costs incurred in connection with the enforcement of a judgment arising from such action or proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: In any action brought under this Agreement, the judge will decide which party is the 'prevailing party' for the purpose of determining who is entitled to recover its costs and attorney's fees. The prevailing party can recover costs only if the judge allows it. Attorney's fees for enforcement of a judgement arising from such action or proceeding will be capped at $5,000.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces the entitlement to recover its *actual costs and attorney fees* with a judge deciding which party is the prevailing party, removing their certainty and adding an attorney's fees cap of $5,000.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1717 establishes the right to recover attorney's fees in contract actions where the contract provides for it. By making attorney's fee award discretionary and setting a fee cap, the modified text undermines the intent and application of this statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1717 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Civil Code § 1717, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, because it makes the award of attorney's fees discretionary ('only if the judge allows it') and imposes a cap ($5,000). The law states the prevailing party 'shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees', indicating a right, not a discretionary allowance. By capping the attorney's fees and making the recovery of costs discretionary, the modified contract undermines the legal entitlement to reasonable attorney's fees established by the statute.
